lesson.name: Language Acquisition - Lesson 5 — Multilingualism and third language acquisition

tasks.chapterTitle: Chapter 1: Cognitive processes and interpreting expertise
Chapter 2: On the acquisition of European Portuguese liquid consonants

messages.content:
This section provides an introduction to the interdisciplinary study of language acquisition, focusing on the cognitive and developmental complexities involved in learning third (L3) or additional languages.

### **Key Points: Language Acquisition**

*   **Growing Complexity:** Globalization has intensified interest in L3/Ln acquisition. Unlike second language (L2) acquisition, research increasingly highlights that L3 acquisition is a distinct area of study influenced by prior linguistic experience and diverse cognitive variables (e.g., aptitude, intelligence, motivation).
*   **Cognitive Processes:**
    *   **Working Memory (WM) & Selective Attention:** These are critical executive functions for complex linguistic tasks. For instance, conference interpreting requires intense, constant training to move from attention-demanding processing to automatic, skilled performance.
    *   **Skill Acquisition Stages:** Described by Anderson (1995) as moving from a *cognitive stage* (developing declarative knowledge), to an *associative stage* (identifying/eliminating errors), and finally an *autonomous stage* (procedural knowledge becomes automatic).
*   **Phonological Development:** Learners from different linguistic backgrounds (e.g., L1-Mandarin speakers) face specific challenges when acquiring phonological properties of a target language (e.g., liquid consonants in European Portuguese).
*   **Methodological Insights in Research:**
    *   Studies on interpreting expertise emphasize that the *quality* of self-study and targeted practice is more significant than the total time spent exercising.
    *   Effective training methods often involve memory enhancement tools like visualization, categorization, and storytelling, as well as specific practice techniques like shadowing and sight translation.
*   **Research Scope:** The field employs diverse methodologies, including longitudinal studies, psychological testing, and qualitative analyses of student habits, aiming to better understand how multilingual education can be tailored to meet students' varied needs.

***

**Source Reference:**
*   [S.9], [S.10]: *Preface* by Jorge Pinto & Nélia Alexandre
*   [S.15], [S.16], [S.17], [S.18], [S.19], [S.20], [S.21]: *Chapter 1: Cognitive processes and interpreting expertise* by Serena Ghiselli

Lesson 5 — Multilingualism and third language acquisition - Presentation5 slides
Multilingualism and Third Language Acquisition
  • Explores learning and teaching trends in multilingual contexts (p. 2).
  • Focuses on L3/Ln acquisition and its unique complexities (p. 9).
  • Features international contributions on cognitive processes and pedagogy (p. 10).

This document is the front matter and table of contents for the book *Multilingualism and Third Language Acquisition: Learning and Teaching Trends*, edited by Jorge Pinto and Nélia Alexandre and published by Language Science Press in 2021 [S.1, S.4].

Key points of the text:

1. Publication Details: The book is the second volume in the "Contact and Multilingualism" series. It is available under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 license and accessible via the Language Science Press website [S.2, S.4].

2. Structural Organization: The content is divided into three main sections:
- Language Acquisition: Covers cognitive processes in interpreting and the acquisition of specific linguistic features (e.g., Portuguese liquid consonants by Mandarin speakers) [S.5].
- Language Teaching: Focuses on pedagogical models for third language instruction, the role of multilingual teachers, the "target-language-only" rule, and teacher training for multilingual environments [S.5].
- Language Learning: Investigates student attitudes in primary school, the development of crosslinguistic awareness, and the impact of plurilingual teachers in higher education [S.6].

3. Acknowledgments and Editorial Support: The editors acknowledge the financial support of the Portuguese Science Foundation (FCT) and express gratitude to the contributors, the series editors, the production staff at Language Science Press, and the various peer reviewers who provided feedback throughout the editorial process [S.7].

This text, the preface to "Multilingualism and third language acquisition: Learning and teaching trends," explores the complexities of language acquisition and current trends in the field of multilingualism.

Key thematic areas include:

The Limitations of Current Theory: The authors note that while decades of research across disciplines like linguistics, psychology, and psycholinguistics have advanced our understanding, no single theory completely explains the language learning process. Acquisition is influenced by a combination of contextual variables and individual biological differences, such as cognitive styles, aptitude, and motivation (p. 9).

The Shift Toward Multilingualism: Globalization has necessitated a transition in research focus from strictly Second Language (L2) acquisition to Third Language or Additional Language (L3/Ln) acquisition. This shift acknowledges that multilingual acquisition is distinct from and more complex than L2 acquisition (p. 9).

Structure and Contributions of the Volume: The book provides an international, multi-perspective approach to language study, organized into three primary sections:

1. Theoretical Foundations: Part I investigates cognitive processes in L3 acquisition, including studies on conference interpreters' working memory and the phonological development of specific learner groups (p. 10).

2. Pedagogical Applications: Part II bridges the gap between research and the classroom. It examines how prior language backgrounds and teacher profiles influence multilingual learning, while advocating for crosslinguistic pedagogy over the traditional monolingual classroom bias (p. 10-11).

3. Learning Strategies: Part III focuses on the individual learner, covering topics such as motivation, crosslinguistic awareness, and how students perceive the role of "plurilingual non-nativist" teachers (p. 11).

The volume concludes that these findings are essential for navigating increasingly diverse classrooms. By analyzing numerous languages—including, among others, Portuguese, Mandarin Chinese, English, and Spanish—the contributors offer a resource for teachers and researchers dedicated to evolving educational strategies in a globalized, multilingual society (p. 11).

This document section explores the role of autonomous exercise in developing cognitive processes, specifically working memory (WM) and selective attention, among conference interpreting students. The study, conducted by Serena Ghiselli at the University of Bologna, aims to bridge the gap in empirical literature regarding self-study habits (p. 15).

Key insights include:

Theoretical Framework
Skill acquisition is characterized by a transition from novice, attention-demanding processing to skilled, automatic processing across three stages: cognitive, associative, and autonomous (p. 17). Proficiency in interpreting requires the efficient use of WM to decode information under cognitive load, coordinated with an attention-switching system (p. 17). 

Impact of Self-Study
While expertise is hard-earned through targeted practice, existing research indicates that the quality of self-study is more critical than its quantity (p. 16, 18). Previous studies, such as those by Fan (2012) and Wang (2016), highlight the necessity of combining quantitative data (frequency/duration) with qualitative data (type of activity) to understand learner habits (p. 18).

Cognitive Training Exercises
Exercises are categorized into three domains to enhance interpreting performance:

Memory Improvement: Techniques include "idiomatic gist" for discourse analysis, and the use of imagination, association, and location (p. 19). Specific exercises like storytelling, categorization, generalization, and parataxis help organize information into manageable schemes, reducing omissions and improving recall (p. 19-20).

Consecutive Interpreting: Essential practices include paraphrasing and monolingual interpreting. Advanced training involves taking notes only after a speech ends or, conversely, practicing without notes to push memory limits (p. 20).

Simultaneous Interpreting: Training focuses on dividing attention. Common exercises include sight translation, shadowing (phonemic, adjusted lag, or phrase-based), and "on-line cloze" tasks. More complex techniques, such as answering questions while listening (Two questions at a time) or within-language paraphrasing, serve to sharpen divided attention and anticipation skills (p. 20-21).

Study Methodology
The project analyzed students from two academic cohorts (starting 2015 and 2016). Data were collected via monthly surveys to track autonomous practice habits without causing participant burnout, providing a longitudinal view on how these students develop the professional skills necessary for interpreting (p. 16, 22).

This section focuses on the study of autonomous study habits among conference interpreting students, tracking their exercise patterns over the course of their master's degree.

Key findings include:

General Study Habits:
Students spend an average of approximately 88 minutes per day on autonomous exercises (p. 14, 18). Exercise frequency and duration changed based on the academic timeline, with intensity significantly increasing during final exam periods to an average of 131.94 minutes due to high pressure (p. 14).

Types of Exercise:
Data analysis categorized activities into simultaneous interpreting, consecutive interpreting, sight translation, and recall (p. 13-14). Consecutive and simultaneous interpreting remained constant or increased over time, while recall exercises—considered a preparatory activity—decreased as students advanced in their training (p. 14, 17).

Additional Self-Study:
Beyond the core interpreting exercises, students frequently engaged in supplementary language development. The most reported activities included terminology research (33%), foreign language listening (20%), and reading (13%) (p. 16, 18). Shadowing was also identified as a persistent practice, utilized by students at various stages of their training for roughly 6% of their total study time (p. 17-18).

Methodological Considerations:
The student-reported data suggests that interpreting students rarely perform formal training exercises on more than 40% of the days surveyed (p. 14, 18). This discrepancy between expected practice and reported behavior may be attributed to the high volume of classroom training, which limits time and mental energy for additional self-study (p. 18).

Future Directions:
The author notes that while linguistic support exercises are common, more challenging cognitive exercises like "two questions at a time" or more complex paraphrasing tasks are either absent or underutilized (p. 18-19). Future research could experimentally determine if integrating these specific, targeted exercises enhances interpreting performance compared to standard preparatory methods (p. 19).

The section spanning pages 49–68 focuses on the pedagogical implications of how a learner's background in previous languages affects the acquisition of a third language (L3). The key points are as follows:

1. The critical role of acquisition context: The chapter by Ana Carvalho investigates whether a uniform pedagogical model can effectively address the needs of diverse learners. It specifically examines how the context in which previous languages (L2s) were acquired—such as learning a language as a heritage language versus as a second language—significantly impacts the learning process of a subsequent target language.

2. Case study of L3 Portuguese: The research highlights distinct differences between bilingual English-Spanish speakers learning Portuguese as an L3. It contrasts those who acquired Spanish as an L2 against those who acquired it as a heritage language. The study reveals that these varying backgrounds lead to divergent classroom performances and different perceptions of the language learning experience.

3. Pedagogical implications: The findings suggest that educators must move away from "one size fits all" teaching models. Instead, pedagogy should be tailored to account for the learner's linguistic history and the specific mechanisms through which their previous languages were internalized, as these factors play a substantial role in shaping their L3 proficiency and learning trajectory.

4. Integration of theory and practice: This section serves to bridge the gap between abstract acquisition theory and the practical challenges faced by teachers in multilingual classrooms, emphasizing that acknowledging the diversity of the student’s linguistic background is essential for effective L3 instruction.

This section (pp. 69–138) focuses on the practical application of language acquisition research to pedagogical strategies and teacher training in multilingual settings.

Key points include:

The role of multilingual teachers and plurilingual approaches: Emel Kucukali (p. 69) explores how multilingual teachers—specifically those proficient in languages like English, German, and Russian—utilize their background to foster L3 acquisition. The study highlights that these teachers act as vital facilitators in the classroom, positively impacting student engagement with plurilingual practices.

Challenging monolingual teaching norms: Pierre-Luc Paquet and Nina Woll (p. 95) investigate the "target-language-only" rule. Their experimental study of student teachers from Canada and Mexico highlights the gap between traditional monolingual classroom biases and the benefits of a crosslinguistic pedagogy that leverages a student's full linguistic repertoire.

Teacher professional development: Julia Barnes and Margareta Almgren (p. 117) discuss the critical need for specialized training for teachers working in diverse, multilingual educational environments. They emphasize that preparing educators for these specific challenges is essential to address the complex needs of students learning additional languages.

Overall, this section argues that pedagogy must move away from rigid, restrictive language policies toward models that actively integrate the previous linguistic knowledge and cultural experiences of both the teacher and the student.

This section covers Part III of the volume, which focuses on language learning within multilingual contexts:

Reflections on Learner Attitudes and Awareness

Barbara Hofer examines student attitudes at the primary school level, conducting an empirical analysis of how learners perceive multilingual environments (p. 139). Gisela Mayr investigates how students build crosslinguistic awareness, emphasizing techniques for bridging languages within multilingual learning settings to foster a deeper understanding of language systems (p. 165).

Perspectives on Plurilingual Educators

Patchareerat Yanaprasart and Sílvia Melo-Pfeifer explore the role of plurilingual non-native teachers in higher education (p. 185). Their research considers whether students perceive these teachers as an added or a mudded value, analyzing the impact of a teacher's own multilingual background on the instructional experience and student perception.

The section concludes with an index of terms to assist with research and reference (p. 207). Overall, these chapters highlight the significance of learner and teacher attitudes in determining the success of pedagogical strategies in diverse language classrooms.

The provided document excerpt, which includes the preface and front matter of a book on multilingualism and third language (L3) acquisition, centers on the following key points:

Research Scope: The volume explores the complexities of third language acquisition (L3/Ln), moving beyond established second language (L2) acquisition theories to address the unique challenges and variables inherent in multilingual learning and teaching.

Academic Context: Edited by Jorge Pinto and Nélia Alexandre, the work serves as a comprehensive collection of theoretical and empirical research featuring contributors from various international academic institutions. The studies are organized into three primary categories: language acquisition, language teaching, and language learning.

Key Research Areas: The book addresses diverse topics, including the impact of cognitive processes (such as working memory and selective attention) on specialized language tasks like interpreting, as well as specific linguistic challenges in phonological acquisition. Furthermore, it examines pedagogical strategies, focusing on how prior language acquisition contexts, teacher training, and plurilingual attitudes influence classroom dynamics and student success.

Core Objectives: A central goal of the volume is to bridge the gap between abstract acquisition models and practical classroom applications. It highlights how the heterogeneous and evolving nature of multilingual education requires nuanced teaching strategies, particularly regarding the use of crosslinguistic awareness versus traditional monolingual instruction.
Here is a quiz for this lesson.

questions.question: According to the preface, why is it challenging to reach a comprehensive, unified view of how second languages are learned?
options: Because there is a lack of academic interest in the field of linguistics.
Because of variations in acquisition contexts and individual biological factors.
Because most learners primarily speak only one language.
Because recent models have concluded that language acquisition cannot be studied scientifically.
questions.explanation: The preface highlights that learning/teaching processes are complex due to variations in learning contexts (input/strategies) and individual student characteristics like age, aptitude, motivation, and cognitive styles.

questions.question: What is the primary focus of Serge Ghiselli's study described in Chapter 1 regarding interpreter training?
options: The impact of native language background on interpreting speed.
The relationship between autonomous self-study habits and improvements in specific cognitive processes.
The physical health benefits of working as an interpreter.
The history of translation studies at the University of Bologna.
questions.explanation: Ghiselli's study explores how the time devoted to exercise and the type of self-study activities contribute to the improvement of working memory and selective attention in conference interpreting students.

questions.question: In the context of skill acquisition, what characterizes the 'autonomous stage' as defined by Anderson (1995)?
options: The memorization of declarative knowledge and new facts.
The identification and elimination of basic mistakes.
The process where learned procedures become more automatic.
The reliance on external feedback to perform a task.
questions.explanation: Anderson's model describes the autonomous stage as the point where procedures become increasingly automatic as declarative knowledge is transformed into procedural knowledge.

questions.question: What is the main finding or observation mentioned regarding the quality of self-study for students of interpreting?
options: Quantity of time spent studying is the only predictor of professional success.
Self-study is unnecessary if the student undergoes formal classroom training.
The quality of self-study is generally more important than the quantity of time spent.
Students typically do not engage in enough self-study to measure results.
questions.explanation: The text notes that literature suggests that targeted exercise is key to automatizing practice and that the quality of self-study is more relevant than its quantity.

questions.question: Why, according to the editors, are studies on Third Language Acquisition (L3/Ln) considered a new and distinct area of research?
options: Because they focus specifically on the differences between acquiring an L3 versus an L2.
Because they prove that multilingualism is biologically impossible for adults.
Because they completely replace the need for theories on second language acquisition.
Because L3 acquisition occurs only in classroom environments, unlike L2 acquisition.
questions.explanation: The preface states that research in this field emphasizes the differences between L2 and L3/Ln acquisition, leading to the establishment of third language acquisition as its own field of study.

cards.front: What are the two main focuses of the articles in the book 'Multilingualism and third language acquisition: Learning and teaching trends'?
cards.back: Language acquisition and language learning/teaching in multilingual settings.

cards.front: What is the cognitive stage of skill acquisition, according to Anderson (1995)?
cards.back: The stage where novices develop declarative knowledge by memorizing elements relevant to the ability.

cards.front: What is the associative stage of skill acquisition, according to Anderson (1995)?
cards.back: The stage where novices gradually identify and eliminate mistakes.

cards.front: What is the autonomous stage of skill acquisition, according to Anderson (1995)?
cards.back: The stage where procedures become more and more automatic as declarative knowledge is turned into procedural knowledge.

cards.front: Why is working memory (WM) crucial in conference interpreting?
cards.back: It is the short-term memory that actively decodes and stores information during complex cognitive activities, which must be trained to handle cognitive load.

cards.front: According to the preface, what shift has occurred in linguistic research since the beginning of the 21st century?
cards.back: There has been a growth of interest in multilingualism and a proliferation of studies on the acquisition of a third or additional language (L3/Ln).

cards.front: What hypothesis does Serena Ghiselli test in her research regarding interpreter training?
cards.back: That the time devoted to exercise and the type of activities done during self-study contribute to the improvement of working memory and selective attention.

cards.front: How do researchers define an expert in a specific domain?
cards.back: Somebody who has achieved a high level of performance and skill as a result of experience and constant, targeted effort.

cards.front: What role do multilingual teachers play in multilingual classrooms according to Kucukali (Chapter 4)?
cards.back: They act as models for plurilingual approaches, playing an important role in the success of multilingual learning and the responsiveness of students.

cards.front: What does 'crosslinguistic pedagogy' prioritize over a 'classroom monolingual bias'?
cards.back: The utilization of a student's full linguistic repertoire (including L1) in the classroom rather than enforcing a target-language-only rule.

slides.title: Multilingualism and Third Language Acquisition
bullets: Explores learning and teaching trends in multilingual contexts (p. 2).
Focuses on L3/Ln acquisition and its unique complexities (p. 9).
Features international contributions on cognitive processes and pedagogy (p. 10).
slides.notes: This volume examines the growth of multilingualism and how L3 acquisition differs from L2, set against the backdrop of globalized language learning.

slides.title: Part I: Language Acquisition
bullets: Covers core theoretical and empirical language acquisition topics (p. 10).
Includes studies on phonology, working memory, and attention (p. 10).
Examines cognitive foundations for advanced language users (p. 10).
slides.notes: Part I establishes the cognitive and developmental foundations of language acquisition, focusing on specific skills required for proficiency.

slides.title: Cognitive Processes in Interpreting
bullets: Investigates working memory and selective attention in students (p. 15).
Hypothesizes that targeted practice improves cognitive performance (p. 15).
Analyzes autonomous study habits over multiple academic years (p. 25).
Shows simultaneous interpreting practice increases during training (p. 26).
slides.notes: Ghiselli explores how consistent, targeted practice helps master's students develop the cognitive expertise necessary for professional interpreting.

slides.title: Part II: Language Teaching
bullets: Explores applying acquisition research to pedagogical issues (p. 10).
Examines how prior learning contexts influence L3 teaching (p. 10).
Discusses plurilingual approaches and teacher belief systems (p. 10).
Addresses tools needed for managing multilingual classrooms (p. 11).
slides.notes: These chapters bridge the gap between acquisition theory and actual classroom practice, emphasizing the role of the teacher in multilingual settings.

slides.title: Part III: Language Learning
bullets: Analyzes student learning strategies and attitudes (p. 11).
Investigates crosslinguistic awareness in multilingual settings (p. 11).
Highlights the dynamic nature of multilingual development (p. 11).
slides.notes: The final section focuses on the learner's perspective, including how individual strategies and attitudes shape the development of multilingual competence.
